---
title: "Eriksen Flanker Experiment"
author: "Rubee Kung, Nicole Hua, Jacob Grima, Eda Kubra Ozkaya"
date: today
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: moon   
    slide-number: true
    transition: fade
    incremental: true
---

## Introduction
- Developed by Barbara A. Eriksen & Charles W. Eriksen (1974)
- Measures selective attention & response inhibition
- Congruent: flankers match target → faster responses
- Incongruent: flankers conflict → slower, more errors

## Why It's Relevant
- Shows how distractions interfere with attention
- Used to study cognitive control & conflict processing
- Linked to brain activity (e.g., conflict monitoring systems)
- Applied in research on ADHD, anxiety, and decision-making

## Methods
- Design
    - Within-subject design
- Conditions:
    - Congruent vs. Incongruent
    - Left vs. Right
- Measures
    - Reaction time (ms)
    - Accuracy (%)

## Procedure
- Procedure
  - 4 practice trials (with feedback)
  - 20 experimental trials (no feedback)
  - Randomized across conditions
- Trial Structure
  - Fixation (500 ms)
  - Stimulus (≤ 2000 ms)
  - Inter-trial interval (700 ms)

## Task & Implementation
- Stimuli & Response
  - Arrow strings (e.g., <<<<<, >><>>)
  - Respond to the center arrow
- Implementation
  - Python (pygame)
  - Recorded RT and accuracy automatically

## Results 
- For our Flanker task, we ran a repeated measures ANOVA
    - Independent variable: congruency 
    - Dependent variable: response time 
- Removed inaccurate trials
- Our ANOVA gave us F(1,15) = 56.70, p<.001

## ANOVA Figure 
![](ANOVAfigure.png)

## Graph Figure 
![](slidesfigure.png)
- Mean rt congruent trials: 477.41 ms
- Mean rt incongruent trials: 571.28 ms 

## Discussion
- The results show that there are differences in response time depending on the congruency of the trials 
- Incongruent trials had longer reaction times than congruent trials 
- We reject the null hypothesis 

## Conclusion
- Arrow congruency significantly affects response time, supporting the flanker interference effect
- Our findings align with the original Eriksen & Eriksen (1974) studies in direction, magnitude (~87ms interference effect), and statistical strength (F > 56)
- Future research could explore individual differences in cognitive control between different demographics
